title: Structs and Pointers
description: Combining memory efficiency with custom types.

slides.title: A Heavy Struct 🏋️
slides.content:
## Passing Heavy Data

Imagine you have a `User` struct with a Name, Age, 500 friends, and a huge profile picture.

If you pass this `User` into a function directly, Go will physically try to copy EVERYTHING. This creates a massive slowdown and wastes huge amounts of computer memory.

quizData.question: Because structs can hold massive amounts of data, passing them exactly directly into functions can actively slow down your Go application over time.
quizData.explanation: Structs directly clone all contained data explicitly, creating vast, heavy memory issues silently.

slides.title: Sharing the Address 📍
slides.content:
Instead of passing a gigantic copy of the `User`, we efficiently pass a *Pointer* to the User! 

The pointer is just a tiny, lightweight memory address.

quizData.question: When defining the `updateAge` function, what specifically proves it expects a pointer to a User rather than a copy of a User?
quizData.explanation: Placing an asterisk `*` explicitly directly before the struct-name `User` accurately establishes a strictly typed Pointer exactly.

slides.title: Go is Smart 🧠
slides.content:
## Automatic Dereferencing

Did you notice something weird in the code above?

If `u` is a pointer (an address), shouldn't we have to dereference it using `(*u).Age` first before we can access its fields?

Yes! But Go designers knew this was annoying to type. So, in Go, **you can just directly use `u.Age` and Go will automatically dereference the pointer for you behind the scenes!**

quizData.question: If you have a pointer to a Struct called `userPtr`, you MUST strictly type `(*userPtr).Name` to access the name.
quizData.explanation: Go conveniently actively allows shorthand strictly natively: `userPtr.Name` securely accurately dereferences it seamlessly.
